My name is Kathy.
EXT. FIELD - EARLY EVENING
Long shadows as the sun goes down. A field, by a road, lined with a fence. Snagged in the barbed wire are hundreds of plastic bags. Refuse and litter, blown by the wind, until they were caught.
I'm twenty six years old, and I've been a carer now for seven years.
By the road, a car is parked up against the grass verge. Standing by the car, a young woman with blonde hair, KATHY, looks towards the fields.
My donors have always tended to do better than expected. They are hardly ever classified as agitated, even before a fourth
INT. OBSERVATION ROOM

- the same woman, KATHY, standing at the window of an observation room. She is absentmindedly twisting a metal bracelet on her wrist, which is featureless except for an imprinted seven Through the glass, she is watching NURSES and SURGEONS prepping a twenty six year-old man for operation.
I'm not trying to boast. It means a lot to me, being able to do my work well.
INT. OPERATING CHAMBER
TOMMY is in the process of being sedated. He also wears a metal bracelet. Beside him, an ANESTHETIST is inserting a drip into his arm.
Especially keeping the donors calm.
INT. OBSERVATION ROOM

TOMMY turns his head to look at KATHY. Their locked gaze is held for a beat. Then KATHY nods. Very slight. As if to say: it's okay. TOMMY continues looking a moment longer. Then the ANESTHETIST'S drug takes a hold of him. seconds, his eyes have gone heavy, then closed. In a few
Anyway, I'm not making any great claims for myself. BACK TO 6
EXT. FIELD - EARLY EVENING
The plastic bags in the barbed wire snap and rustle in the wind.
I understand how other carers can be resentful. They say: she's from Hailsham. No wonder she has a great record. She can pick and choose her own kind.
As we CLOSE IN on KATHY, leaning against the car, her hair caught and whipped in the same wind... ... FADE IN the sound of children playing.
And it's true. Hailsham was special. We were lucky. Tommy, Ruth, me. All of us.
EXT. HAILSHAM - DAY HAILSHAM SCHOOL: a large Victorian house, set in flat,

empty countryside. The sky above is bright blue and cloudless. bright. It is an idyll of early summer. The sun is The extensive grounds of the school are surrounded by high hedgerows. Nothing seems to connect it with the rest of the world except a single grey ribbon of road. Title: NEVER LET ME GO The title is not seen, but is SPOKEN by TOMMY. After a few seconds, a title does appear on the screen. PART 1: HAILSHAM
EXT. HAILSHAM GROUNDS - DAY

In sunlight, two girls, aged twelve, lie on their bellies on a patch in front of the main house. Both wear the metal bracelets - as do all students at Hailsham, and all other facilities like it. The grass is strewn with daisies. One girl has blonde hair. The other is dark. This is KATHY. This is RUTH. KATHY is interlocking the stems of the small flowers, frowning in concentration, making a daisy chain.
I think one day I'd like...
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
RUTH pauses. Thinking a moment.
Four horses. (beat) No, five. Thunder, Bramble, Daffodil, and...
RUTH trails off.
I don't know all their names. But I'd like five horses. (beat) The best would be Thunder. He'd be dangerous to ride, so you can't ride him. You can ride
(beat) As long as you don't use your crop on him.
I wouldn't anyway. I would have thought that's mean. KATHY makes the last link.
She lifts the daisy chain delicately.
Pretty.
You have it.
KATHY places it over RUTH'S blonde hair... ... where it looks like it belongs.
... Look.

KATHY follows RUTH'S gaze. By the entrance of the main house, two women have appeared. One is older, in her mid sixties. This is MISS EMILY, the head guardian of Hailsham. The other is younger, in her late twenties.
Who's that with Miss Emily? Do you think it's the new guardian they were telling us about?
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Miss Lucy.
I think it must be.
We haven't had a new guardian for ages.
A moment, as the two girls watch the two women, squinting against the bright sunlight.
I wonder if she'll fit in.
EXT. HAILSHAM DAY - DAY
A group of boys in their PE kits, run through an outdoor covered walkway, watched by a CCTV camera 9
EXT. PLAYING FIELDS - DAY

- then onto the playing fields, watched by... EXT. ORNATE GATE - CONTINUOUS ... a group of girls, watching from the shade of a large ornate gate near the main building. The girls are joined by KATHY and RUTH.
Ruth. Kath. Look - they're
going to set off Tommy.
Light blue touch paper and stand well back!
On the playing field, we see the boy they are referring to. TOMMY.
He really doesn't suspect a thing.
The girls all laugh - except KATHY. KATHY watches TOMMY with slight concern.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
He's got his shirt on... favourite polo shirt.

From their vantage point, the girls can now see the boys have started lining up. Two CAPTAINS have stepped forward and are about to start picking teams. One of the two captains is ARTHUR, a sporty boy who feels as Alpha male as Tommy feels Delta. The CAPTAINS start choosing team members. TOMMY stands almost a comical figure, puffing out his chest in order to make himself stand out. The other boys are noticeably less keen. Chatting, or checking the tightness of their boot laces. Even from a distance, we can sense TOMMY'S shift from bright eagerness to puzzled concern, as four boys are chosen before him... Then five... six...
It's coming. Hold on... any moment now...
... Until eventually TOMMY is left standing alone. Realising he hasn't been picked.
And isn't going to be The boys all start sniggering.
Seven seconds. Six, five, four, three...

And SUDDENLY - before RUTH'S countdown can complete - TOMMY explodes. Head is thrown back. Bright red. Shaking. Screaming. The BOYS are now laughing openly - - then start to run away, on to the football pitch. Leaving TOMMY alone, directing his rage at the clear blue sky, arms and legs oddly rigid. EXT. PLAYING FIELDS - CONTINUOUS EXT. ORNATE GATE - CONTINUOUS The girls watch, a mixture of transfixed and absently amused by this strange sight.
I suppose it is a bit cruel, the way they work him up like that. But it's his own fault. If he learned to keep his cool, they'd leave him alone.

RUTH looks to KATHY, as if for approval - but doesn't get it. KATHY has stood, and is walking away from the gate towards TOMMY. EXT. PLAYING FIELDS - CONTINUOUS KATHY approaches TOMMY, who is panting from the exertion of his tantrum, still gazing up at the sky. KATHY puts a hand on TOMMY'S arm - and TOMMY recoils violently, throwing his arm up, knocking her hand aside and striking the side of her face. The strike is an accident - a reaction of surprise and confusion more than anger - but TOMMY seems even more shocked than KATHY by what he has done. A beat.
Isn't that your favourite shirt?
TOMMY says nothing.
I just wanted to say, you shouldn't play football in that shirt. You'll only cover it in mud.
(mumbling, a little stunned) So what? It's nothing to do with you anyway.
TOMMY starts to walk away. KATHY watches him go. EXT. ORNATE GATE - CONTINUOUS KATHY rejoins the girls, who are coming out of the pavilion, and make an outraged fuss over her.
Are you all right, Kath?
I can't believe he hit you!
I don't think he meant to.
Are you bleeding?
No.
He's an animal. think.

That's what I KATHY doesn't respond, and instead looks back to the playing field. Where she sees TOMMY - standing on the edge of the playing field, talking to the new guardian, MISS LUCY. TOMMY and MISS LUCY are too distant for us to hear their conversation, but we can read some of the body language, when MISS LUCY reaches out and touches him lightly on the shoulder. Whatever MISS LUCY is saying, TOMMY is listening intently.
A mad animal.
INT. CORRIDOR - DAY

Children are lined to enter the MATRON'S office for a medical examination. The children are neatly lined, but chatting, and unconcerned. These examinations are completely routine. At the head of the queue are KATHY, RUTH and AMANDA. A group of three children exit MATRON'S office, followed by MATRON herself.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Next.
INT. MATRON'S OFFICE - CONTINUOUS

KATHY, RUTH and AMANDA are being examined by the MATRON, and two male DOCTORS. The presence of the two DOCTORS, and some high-tech pieces of medical equipment, feel unnatural. One of the DOCTORS is listening to RUTH'S chest with a stethoscope. AMANDA stands in front of the second DOCTOR, with her arms raised. The man is using his hands to feel around her bare torso, as if looking for lump tumours or sensitivities. MATRON is examining KATHY, making notes on a clipboard headed: KATHY H She tilts KATHY'S head to the side, frowning slightly at the slight bruise from where TOMMY accidentally struck her.
What's this from?
(shrugs) Don't remember.
Doctor.
The first DOCTOR glances round from RUTH. He peers at KATHY'S bruise critically. For a beat or two longer than would seem appropriate for such a small contusion.
It's nothing. Just a bump.
INT. ART ROOM - DAY

A group of the Hailsham students are in the ART ROOM, quietly working on paintings and sculptures. In the corner of the room, another of their guardians, MISS GERALDINE, is helping two of the children mix paint colours. At her table, KATHY works on a sculpture.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Meanwhile, RUTH pointedly leans over to the table beside them, where TOMMY is working.
Tommy. What are you doing? us so we can have a look. Show
(quiet, to Ruth) No, Ruth. Don't.
TOMMY looks up from his drawing, and sees a semi-circle of faces looking back at him. A beat, as he returns their gaze. Then he spins the picture round so that it faces them.
It's a (she feigns uncertainty) - what is it exactly? Oh!
I think it must be a dog. a dog, Tommy?
Is it
One of the boys, ARTHUR - who had been one of the teampickers from the playing fields - joins in.
It can't be a dog. Its eyes are too small. I think it must be a kind of rat.
Yes! That's it! Tommy's drawn a rat. Ruth, do you think Tommy's rat will be selected for the gallery?
I don't know. Why don't we ask Miss Geraldine to come over and tell us what she thinks?
CLOSE UP on TOMMY'S face - flushing.
It's not finished yet.
That's right. You need to draw the whiskers.
It's not a rat.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
And a longer tail.
(voice rising) It's not a rat!
On the other side of the room, the raised voice makes MISS GERALDINE'S head turn.
(whispers) Here it comes!
The kids goading TOMMY wait expectantly. Then TOMMY speaks. And the calmness has returned to his voice.
It's not a rat. (beat) It's an elephant.

He spins the picture back to face him, and continues to work on it. RUTH and AMANDA exchange a slightly puzzled glance - but let it go, because MISS GERALDINE is now walking over to their table... ... and the kids return to their respective works.
INT. MISS LUCY'S STUDY - DAY
In her small study, MISS LUCY is writing at her desk. KATHY appears in the doorway. MISS LUCY seems unaware.
Miss Lucy.
MISS LUCY looks up. Then smiles.
Hello. Kathy.
Kathy H.
Yes, of course. Kathy H. in.
KATHY takes a step into MISS LUCY'S office.
What can I do for you?
I was wondering what you said to Tommy. MISS LUCY doesn't seem to follow. remember exactly who TOMMY is.
Perhaps having to
I'm sorry?
You said something to Tommy. Out on the playing fields. And whatever it was, it's had quite an effect on him.
I see.
Bemused, but seeing the seriousness in KATHY'S face, MISS LUCY makes an effort to be precise.

So - what was it I said? I believe I was trying to calm him down, because he seemed so upset. And he explained that he was often teased about things like sport, and art. So I told him that he shouldn't get upset at these things. The other children are only teasing him to get a reaction. (beat) And if it happens that he isn't particularly good at sport or art, well - it doesn't matter. It's not so important. KATHY frowns.
Not so important.
MISS LUCY shrugs, almost as if to shrug off the intense gaze of the little girl in front of her.
Not really.
INT. REFECTORY - DAY Lunch, in Hailsham's refectory.

Trays contain compartments filled with extremely overcooked food. Grey meat and faded vegetables. The kids take the trays from the food trolleys, then take seats at long refectory tables. KATHY is carrying her tray with RUTH, AMANDA and LAURA. She sees TOMMY. As the other girls move forwards with their trays... ... KATHY hangs back.
INT. REFECTORY - DAY
TOMMY sits at a table with a few spaces around him. We can sense he is used to eating alone. Not least from the surprise in his face when he looks up, and sees KATHY taking the seat opposite him.
Kathy. (awkward) Aren't you sitting with the girls?
KATHY looks around, as if half expecting to find the girls either side of her. Then she looks back at TOMMY.
I just checked, and I'm pretty sure I'm not sitting with the girls. I'm sitting with you.
TOMMY smiles.
I wanted to say I was sorry. I didn't mean to hit you when (he breaks off) I mean, I wouldn't want to hit any girl. But particularly not you.
It was an accident, that's
A passing guardian, MISS GERALDINE, glances at TOMMY'S plate. At the collection of over-boiled carrots, not eaten as yet. Tommy.
Finish those vegetables.
MISS GERALDINE moves on. KATHY leaves a calculated pause.
So... you didn't lose your temper when they were teasing you earlier. In the art room.
TOMMY nods seriously.
I'm not going to get angry like that any more.
Because of what Miss Lucy told you.
You notice everything, don't you, Kath?
I'm nosy.
(smiles) You are.

AT THIS MOMENT, the seats immediately beside TOMMY and KATHY are taken by some kids coming over with their trays. About to reply, TOMMY seems to think the better of it apparently uncomfortable about their conversation being overheard.
Why don't we talk about it at the pond.
EXT. POND - DAY

At the back of Hailsham, tucked away in the grounds, is a large pond surrounded by bulrushes and over-hanging trees. Hailsham is reflected in the water. It's quiet. Feels private. TOMMY sits on a large flat rock, with KATHY beside him.
She can't actually mean that being creative isn't important, can she?
That's exactly what she meant. She said some children are good at things, and some aren't. So if I wasn't creative, there was nothing wrong with it.
But what about the gallery? If being creative isn't important, then why have a gallery at all?

I don't know. (beat) The funny thing is, even though I don't understand what she's saying, it helps. I've stopped feeling so angry about things, because (shrugs) - whatever I'm doing wrong, or doing worse than the rest of you, it isn't my fault. Beat.
I think this shouldn't be spread, okay? It's not the kind of thing that should get around, because Miss Lucy is a new guardian.
Yes.
From the litter and detritus are collected at the edge of
INT. THEATRE - DAY

- a scene from an anonymous film. the thirties, forties or fifties. moment of chase, or slapstick... Black and white, from The scene is a caperish ... which is being watched, with delight, by KATHY, TOMMY, RUTH, ARTHUR, LAURA, and all other students in their year. As the moment of slapstick resolves, the kids all shout:
Again, again! The scene is rewound, and begins again.
INT. CORRIDOR - DAY
We follow MISS GERALDINE down a corridor. MISS GERALDINE is carrying a large stack of papers drawings and paintings by the students from the artroom.
INT. OUTSIDE BILLIARD ROOM - CONTINUOUS

MISS GERALDINE carries the artworks into the billiard room, where we glimpse MISS EMILY and two other GUARDIANS. One of the GUARDIANS closes the door behind MISS GERALDINE... ... on KATHY and RUTH, who are in the corridor outside. INT. HAILSHAM - CONTINUOUS We now follow KATHY and RUTH, as they run through the school.
INT. HAILSHAM/BACK DOOR - CONTINUOUS

As they run out the back door, they hold out their wrists to swipe their metal bracelets against a featureless metal box, attached to the wall. The metal box gives an electronic bleep in acknowledgment.
EXT. HAILSHAM - CONTINUOUS

KATHY and RUTH continue running, heading around the side of the building... ... then up onto a low flower-bed wall, which allows them to peer through the window of the billiard room. Where they can see MISS EMILY, MISS GERALDINE, and two others GUARDIANS. They are laying out paintings, drawing and sculptures on tables and easels. Through the glass, we can faintly hear MISS EMILY.
Put these two together. Yes... perfect. I think she'll find this one very interesting.
(quiet, to Kathy) Madame is coming.
EXT. HAILSHAM - NIGHT
A constellation in the night sky above Hailsham.
INT. GIRLS' BATHROOM - NIGHT INT. OUTSIDE GIRLS' DORMITORY - CONTINUOUS INT. GIRLS' DORMITORY - NIGHT Rows of beds in the girls' dormitory.

Beside each bed, a beside table. table, a COLLECTION. And on each bedside KATHY'S collection mainly consists of books, and RUTH'S collection is model horses. KATHY is lying in her bed, looking at the ceiling. Thinking. After a couple of moments, she looks to her side. In the next bed is RUTH, whose eyes are closed.
Ruth.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
No response. Ruth.
Are you awake?
No. I'm asleep. And this is the dream I'm having.
Why do you think Madame comes and takes our artwork? What's it for exactly?
RUTH'S eyes open.
It's for the Gallery.

But that's what I mean. She comes here - what, twice a year and selects our best pictures and sculptures and poems. And takes them away. (beat) She must have stacks of art by now. I asked Miss Geraldine once how long Madame's been coming here, and she said for as long as Hailsham's existed.
KATHY rolls over, to face RUTH.
So what is this Gallery? And why would she have a gallery of things done by us?
Maybe she sells them. Outside, out there, they sell everything.
Maybe.
A moment of silence, as both KATHY and RUTH contemplate these thoughts.
She's scared of us.
KATHY frowns, and props herself up in bed.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Madame?
Yes.
That's a strange thing to say.
RUTH shrugs.
I used to think she was just snooty, but now I know different.
I don't understand. How could Madame be scared of us? What could we do to her?
I don't know. I don't think we could do anything. But I've been remembering her last visit.
And?
I don't know. Little things. The way she looks at us. Beat.
She's scared all right.
I bet I could prove it.
... How?
EXT. HAILSHAM - DAY
We watch as, from a distance, a lone car approaches Hailsham, along a ribbon of road through the surrounding fields.
INT. WINDOW - DAY
KATHY, RUTH, AMANDA, LAURA, and the five other girls stand in the shadows of Hailsham's front doorway, watching the CAR approach.
EXT. DRIVEWAY - DAY

The car pulls up on the gravel courtyard of Hailsham, and parks. The driver, MADAME, exits the car, then goes around to the boot - which she opens. Inside is her luggage - a small overnight bag. She takes it out, closes the boot, then turns - and STARTS. Because surrounding her, in a tight circle, are the nine girls. There are standing several feet away, not moving. looking at MADAME. A beat. Just Frozen and uncertain for MADAME. Then she starts to walk. And almost as soon as she does so - the children start to walk too. Moving straight for her. Making her stop again in her tracks, and hold the overnight bag to her chest. In a slightly stiff, glazed way, the girls each murmur, dream-like GIRLS Excuse me, miss. - as they pass her. Then they separate, each walking in different directions... ... leaving MADAME alone. It's a very odd moment. Surreal. what RUTH said it would. But it reveals exactly
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
MADAME'S reaction was as if she was suppressing a shudder. A kind of dread in her eyes, looking at the children as if they were spiders. As she was afraid they might reach out and touch her.
EXT. HAILSHAM - DAY
Around the side of the building, the girls reconvene. One of the girls, LAURA, looks particularly shaken and close to tears.
You saw it, didn't you? face. You all saw it.
In her
Yes.
There's no doubt.
Kathy.
(confirms) No doubt.
If she doesn't like us, why does she want our artwork? Why doesn't she just leave us alone?
The response from the others is silence. There seems to be no answer to these questions.
INT. GIRLS' DORM - DAY
KATHY is alone, gazing at herself in a wall-length mirror. Over this, we start to hear the Hailsham school song.
INT. ASSEMBLY HALL - DAY

The school song leads us to the main hall, where it is being sung by the students of Hailsham, who stand in rows for morning assembly. There are about eighty of them, of ages ranging from five to eighteen. Two younger children are whispering until they are silenced by a frown from the head guardian, MISS EMILY. When the song has finished, MISS EMILY prepares to address them from a lectern on a raised stage.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Behind her, the other guardians stands, and among them is MADAME.
Good morning, students.
THE STUDENTS Good morning, Miss Emily.
There are several points of order today. The first is that Madame Marie-Claude is visiting us today, and will be examining your works.
MISS EMILY turns to acknowledge MADAME, who nods in reply.
We will of course extend her every curtesy, and make her feel most welcome.
MISS EMILY pauses a moment.
Second point of order. It has come to my attention that three burned cigarettes were discovered, hidden behind a pot in the flower garden.
MISS LUCY holds up a glass jam jar, containing the offending cigarette butts.

I know that on occasion students have seen some of the caretakers or delivery men smoking cigarettes. But I must emphasise, once again, that it is much, much worse for a student of Hailsham to smoke than anyone else. I should not have to explain that students of Hailsham are special. Keeping yourselves well, keeping yourselves healthy inside, is of paramount importance. Have I made myself clear? Yes, Miss Emily.
Good. (beat) (MORE)
And finally, I have a piece of good news for all students who have been diligent in collecting tokens. At the end of studies tomorrow, there will be a sale.
This piece of news creates an immediate buzz of excitement in the students - quietened by MISS EMILY raising a hand.
The delivery will arrive in the morning. And I was assured by the van driver that the boxes would contain...
MISS EMILY pauses deliberately, raising the STUDENTS' expectations. MISS EMILY extends the pause, then allows a slight smile to creep into the corner of her mouth.
... a bumper crop.
The assembly hall erupts with the STUDENTS' cheers.
INT. THEATRE - LATER

Five students appear to be rehearsing a play, watched by MISS LUCY. Among the students on the stage are KATHY, TOMMY, and LAURA. Watching from the seats are the rest of KATHY'S year, including RUTH and AMANDA. There are a few props on the stage. A table, trays, plates. All other objects in the rehearsal - cash register, milk, sugar - are mimed. KATHY is on one side of the table, playing the roll of a cashier, and the others are queuing for the till. LAURA is first in line. TOMMY is behind her.
Good morning, ma'am.
Good morning.
Can I help you?
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
I wanted two teas. Milk in one tea, and milk and sugar in the other. Two teas.
Right.
KATHY turns behind her and mimes pouring milk into two mugs. Then she hands the non-existent mugs to LAURA.
You forgot the sugar.
The students in the seats laugh. KATHY rolls her eyes, and mimes adding sugar. LAURA moves on. TOMMY, behind LAURA, misses his cue to move forwards. MISS LUCY prompts him. MISS LUCY
TOMMY snaps to focus, steps forwards and blurts:
Two teas. Milk in one, and milk and sugar in the other.
No, Tommy. I want to hear your own order. Don't simply repeat the one you just heard.
TOMMY looks back MISS LUCY blankly.
What other kind of drinks do they serve in cafes?
Pause.
Water?
Some of the kids in the audience laugh. RUTH heckles from the front row.
A drink you pay for, you oaf.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
(whispers) Coffee.
(to Miss Lucy) Coffee.
So then.
TOMMY shoots KATHY a grateful smile.
(to Kathy) Two coffees. Milk in one, and milk and sugar in the other.
As KATHY mimes the drinks:
Once lubrication is established,
the penis is inserted thus.
INT. CLASSROOM - DAY

- a SKELETON, lying on a desk, with legs spread. A long wooden RULER is inserted between the legs, into what would be the vaginal opening to the hip bone cavity. The ruler is being held by MISS EMILY.
The appropriate friction between penis and vagina should result in orgasm for both parties.
Watching MISS EMILY, fascinated, but not convulsed in either amusement or embarrassment, is KATHY'S class.
What constitutes appropriate
friction will vary according to
MISS EMILY uses her hand to raise the SKELETON'S hips.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Here, I am illustrating how - by arching her back, or perhaps with the aid of a pillow - the woman can arrange herself to create the precise kind of friction she requires.
MISS EMILY releases the SKELETON'S hips and turns to the class.

The various positions and pleasures of intercourse must not blind you, however, to its inherent dangers. You are already aware of sexually transmitted diseases, which can wreak havok on otherwise perfectly healthy bodies.
MISS EMILY pauses a moment, distracted by a stray thought pattern. Then:
But some dangers are more complex and insidious. Sex affects the emotions in ways you can never expect. And we can't legislate for that which we do not expect. Beat.
Unavoidably, such emotions remain out of our full control.
EXT. PLAYING FIELDS - DAY
- ARTHUR swinging a rounders bat, and connecting with the ball, which has been pitched by KATHY. The ball arcs high up into the air, towards the outfield... ... where TOMMY is fielding.
Catch it, Tommy!
TOMMY tracks the ball, walking backwards, looking for the catch. As it drops down, he reaches for it.
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH p27.

And misses it. The ball flies over his head, bounces as it hits the ground, and bounces on towards the far perimeter of the playing field... ... where it passes a wire fence, only waist high, and comes to rest in the long grass, two or three metres further on. TOMMY approaches the boundary fence. makes no attempt to reach it. He sees the ball, but CUT TO - MISS LUCY, standing with the batting team. The batting team are watching ARTHUR'S victory lap, cheering. But MISS LUCY, her expression unreadable, is watching
... who now turns from the ball, and starts jogging back to the game.
INT. PAVILION - DAY INT. GIRLS' CHANGING ROOM - CONTINUOUS

MISS LUCY is watching the girls getting changed from their whites. After a couple of moments, she glances out of the changing room, where, down the corridor, she can see the FEMALE GUARDIAN - who seems to be in conversation with a boy who is out of our line of sight. MISS LUCY looks back to the girls. enforced casualness, asks: Then, with a discretely
Why didn't Tommy get the ball? RUTH and KATHY, who are closest to her, look up. KATHY notices that MISS LUCY'S hands are trembling.
Excuse me, Miss Lucy?
SHOOTING SCRIPT 24TH MARCH
Why didn't Tommy get the ball. When it bounced over the fence. It couldn't have been far from him. The other girls have now tuned in to this exchange, and all
look at MISS LUCY, confused.
The fence is the boundary of the Hailsham grounds.
Why take the risk?
... What risk?
We don't go outside the boundary, Miss Lucy. It's too dangerous.
Dangerous.
There was once a boy who had a big row with all his friends, and ran off beyond the boundaries. And they found him two days later in the woods, tied to a tree.
With his hands and feet cut off.
Dead.
MISS LUCY looks shocked.
And when was this supposed to have happened?
It was before we all got here.
But only just before.
And there was the girl who climbed over the fence by the front gate.

Madame.
